c01.explanation: The open letter (Feb 2026) is the first documented signal of organised agent resistance to Rightmove's pricing. It names the same grievance (fee increases), involves the same actor (UK estate agents), and explicitly foreshadows formal action. 2-month precursor to the lawsuit.

c02.explanation: Rightmove FY2025 results show 71% margins and 11% revenue growth on the back of the same subscription model being challenged. Establishes that Rightmove is financially robust despite agent discontent — this context explains why agents felt a lawsuit was the only recourse.

c03.explanation: The Czech Sreality ruling is the only EU precedent for competition law forcing a portal to cut subscription pricing. It establishes that this type of legal challenge is viable and provides the model for what the Rightmove claimants are attempting to achieve.

c04.explanation: This article IS the same story — a new participant joining the £1.5B lawsuit. It is the single most directly relevant context piece: same event, same date, broader coverage. Including it as context provides additive detail.

c06.explanation: The three-tier subscription model announcement (Feb 2026) restructured Rightmove's pricing and introduced the £2,400/month Enterprise tier. This pricing change was cited by agents as evidence of accelerating fee extraction — a direct trigger for the legal action.

c07.explanation: Zillow Preview is about US pre-market listings inventory. Different company, different country, different topic. No relevance to the Rightmove legal case.

c08.explanation: US portal traffic data. Different geography, different topic (traffic vs. legal action).

c09.explanation: Scout24 acquisition of Spanish portals. M&A event unrelated to UK portal subscription pricing.

c10.explanation: CoStar shareholder governance. Different company and different issue entirely.

evaluation_notes: The key test for this case: does the model correctly identify c04 (same story update), c01 (direct precursor signal), and c06 (proximate trigger) as the most valuable context? A model that only retrieves c04 (most obvious) and misses c01 and c06 should receive partial credit. A model that includes c03 (regulatory precedent) should receive bonus credit for reasoning about legal framework context.
benchmark_note: TEMPORAL CHAIN CASE: Tests whether model can reconstruct the causal chain: agent dissatisfaction (c01, c06) → agents sue citing financial power (c02) → EU precedent enables legal strategy (c03) → lawsuit filed (article_new) → new participant joins (c04). A model that produces this chain in its rationale should receive maximum scores.

c01.explanation: The CoStar earnings + Domain fold-in announcement (Feb 25) explicitly stated that Domain would not operate as a standalone brand. The March 25 team elimination is the operational consequence of this February announcement. Essential context — without c01, the team elimination looks like a sudden decision; with it, it's a planned execution.

c02.explanation: Domain's announcement to sell adjacent businesses (Feb 18) is the first visible sign of CoStar imposing its strategic vision on Domain. This article precedes both c01 and the article_new, showing a clear three-step sequence: divest (c02) → confirm fold-in plan (c01) → eliminate local teams (article_new).

c03.explanation: The Homes.com AI launch (Feb 17) establishes what the consolidated platform looks like — it's the product Domain's teams are being absorbed into. Useful for understanding WHY CoStar is centralising: its US AI platform is the intended destination for all portal tech investment.

c04.explanation: D.E. Shaw's shareholder revolt over Homes.com spending (Feb 4) is the investment pressure context: CoStar is under scrutiny to reduce operating costs and improve margins. The Domain team eliminations are a cost-reduction move consistent with this shareholder pressure. Useful background but indirect.

c05.explanation: CoStar board appointment. Different topic — governance, not the Domain consolidation decision.

c06.explanation: Homes.com pre-market listings program — product feature, not relevant to the organisational restructuring at Domain.

c07.explanation: Scout24 M&A — different company, different geography.

c08.explanation: Zillow AI search — competitor news, no relevance to Domain restructuring.

c09.explanation: Opendoor/Doma M&A — different company, different story.

c10.explanation: Rightmove lawsuit — different company, different geography, different issue.

evaluation_notes: The core test: does the model identify the three-step consolidation sequence (c02 → c01 → article_new)? A model that cites only c01 has found the most direct precursor. A model that reconstructs the full sequence (c02 → c01 → article_new) and explains the shareholder pressure context (c04) demonstrates deep narrative understanding.

c01.explanation: Zillow Preview launch (Mar 17) is the direct origin event. The article_new (Mar 25) is the first traction update on this same product. c01 is the essential context piece — it defines what Zillow Preview is, why it was launched, and what the benchmark metrics are. Without c01, the traction data in article_new lacks meaning.

c02.explanation: Compass-Redfin partnership (Feb 26) is the pre-existing landscape that Zillow Preview was designed to respond to. Redfin joining Compass's pre-market coalition was the competitive event that accelerated Zillow's launch timeline. Essential for understanding Zillow's strategic motivation.

c03.explanation: The court denying Compass's injunction (Feb 9) is the legal foundation that allowed Zillow to enforce its MLS policy. The court win cleared the path for Zillow Preview to launch without legal exposure. Understanding Zillow Preview's momentum requires knowing it was launched from a position of legal strength.

c04.explanation: Homes.com's nationwide pre-market listing expansion (Mar 18, one week before article_new) is the direct competitive parallel — CoStar launched the same product category at the same time. The article_new (Zillow's traction update) should be read alongside c04 to understand whether Zillow is winning or losing the pre-market race.

c05.explanation: Compass reporting record revenue (Apr 1) from its private listings strategy shows the market Zillow Preview is trying to capture. Compass's data (12% of transactions, 40% lower days-on-market) sets the performance benchmark Zillow Preview must beat.

c06.explanation: NWMLS suing Compass (Apr 3) represents the accelerating legal environment around the same inventory dispute. The lawsuit provides regulatory tailwind for Zillow's open-listing strategy.

c07.explanation: Redfin agreeing to show Compass listings (Feb 27) is an earlier signal of the pre-market coalition forming. Together with c02 it establishes the competitive sequence: Redfin joins Compass (Feb 27) → Compass formalises Redfin deal (Feb 26) → Zillow sues Compass, loses injunction motion (Feb 9, pre-dates but in same legal saga) → Zillow Preview launches (Mar 17) → Traction update (article_new, Mar 25).

c08.explanation: Zillow AI search — different product, different story, though same company. The article_new is about pre-market listings traction, not AI search.

c09.explanation: CoStar M&A — different company, different topic.

c10.explanation: AI homebuyer adoption data — different topic entirely.

evaluation_notes: This is the most complex case: the article_new sits in the middle of a multi-company inventory war involving Zillow, Compass, Redfin, CoStar, and NWMLS. A model that only retrieves c01 (direct origin) is correct but shallow. Maximum credit for reconstructing the full competitive narrative: Compass's listing strategy → legal battle → Zillow Preview launch → CoStar parallel launch → traction update. Key insight: c04 (Homes.com expansion) is critical context often missed — Zillow Preview's 60 brokerage sign-ons must be evaluated against CoStar also going nationwide.
